data.title: Apple picks up pace in replacing hardware with in-house solutions: Kuo
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) continues to implement more in-house hardware into its iPhones, as TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo said Apple now plans to replace Broadcom's (NASDAQ:AVGO) Wi-Fi chips in all of its iPhone 17 models.

This follows Apple's confirmation on Wednesday that the iPhone 16e would have an in-house modem chip, known as the C1. Qualcomm (QCOM) has provided the modem chips for Apple devices for more than a decade.

"Following Qualcomm, Broadcom's Wi-Fi chips will also be replaced by Apple's in-house chips at a faster pace," Kuo posted on X on Thursday. "My latest industry survey indicates that all new 2H25 iPhone 17 models will feature Apple's in-house Wi-Fi chips (vs. only the slim iPhone 17 will adopt Apple's C1 modem chip). Beyond cost reduction, the switch to in-house Wi-Fi chips will enhance connectivity across Apple devices."

It's important to note that even though Apple is building its own modems and Wi-Fi chips for more of its iPhones, this hardware is often already patented. For example, even though Apple has replaced Qualcomm modems with their own in some iPhone models, they are utilizing a patent agreement. This means Qualcomm can still receive royalty payments even if it isn't building the C1.

Broadcom and Qualcomm were both down about 1% during early afternoon trading on Thursday. Apple inched up half a percent.

data.title: Leonardo’s shares jump on defense orders, cost cuts
data.description:
Leonardo DRS (NASDAQ:DRS) on Thursday rose as much as 9.7% after the Italian aerospace and defense company beat estimates with higher orders and a plan to reduce costs.

Revenue jumped 11% from a year earlier to 17.76 billion euros ($18.51 billion) in 2024, exceeding the company-compiled consensus of €16.8 billion.

Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes and amortization was €1.525 billion, beating the company-compiled consensus of €1.44 billion.

Orders rose 12% from a year earlier to €20.945 billion, beating market expectations for €19.5 billion.

The company, which is a supplier to Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF) and Boeing (BA), saw the biggest gain in its Defense Electronics and Security unit with orders valued at €10.33 billion. In second place was its Helicopters group with €5.87 billion in orders.

Leonardo (NASDAQ:DRS) is among the European defense contractors whose sales and orders have improved as the region supplies military support to Ukraine and rebuilds armed forces.

Supply-chain constraints that have dogged much of the aerospace and defense industry started to ease. Leonardo (DRS) said a cost-cutting program also helped results.

The company’s Aerostructures division posted a loss in ebita terms of €151 million, little changed from a year earlier. Revenue rose 17% while orders gained 7.5% from a year earlier as Boeing (BA) prepared to increase output.

Leonardo’s (DRS) updated industrial plan will be published March 11.

data.title: Shake Shack projects $205M-$215M adjusted EBITDA for FY 2025 with 22% restaurant margin target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Shake Shack Inc. (NYSE:SHAK) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Rob Lynch highlighted Shake Shack's strong Q4 2024 performance, with revenue increasing 14.8% year-over-year, driven by a 4.3% same-Shack sales growth. Restaurant-level margins expanded to 22.7%, a 290-basis-point improvement, while adjusted EBITDA grew nearly 50%. The company reiterated its long-term goal of expanding to at least 1,500 company-operated Shacks across the U.S.

  • The company outlined its six 2025 strategic priorities, including leadership development programs, operational optimizations, and guest frequency initiatives. Innovative programs like the "Shift Up" development program resulted in 100% retention of graduates and nearly one-third promoted to managerial roles.

  • CFO Katie Fogertey emphasized record profitability for 2024, with adjusted EBITDA reaching $175.6 million (33% growth year-over-year) and restaurant-level profit margins expanding 150 basis points to 21.4% for the year. She noted that Q4 restaurant-level profit was $71.9 million, or 22.7% of Shack sales.

Outlook

  • Management provided FY 2025 revenue guidance of $326.5 million to $330.9 million for Q1, with same-Shack sales growth of 2.5% to 3.5%. Full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance was raised to $205 million to $215 million, reflecting 17% to 22% growth year-over-year.

  • The company targets approximately 22% restaurant-level margins in 2025, supported by improved labor management, supply chain efficiencies, and other operational initiatives. Pricing strategies remain modest, with in-Shack pricing expected to rise 2% year-over-year and overall pricing up 3% across all channels.

Financial Results

  • Shake Shack reported Q4 2024 revenue of $328.7 million, a 15% increase year-over-year. Same-Shack sales grew by 4.3%, driven by 4.8% price mix, although traffic slightly declined due to weather and infill-related headwinds.

  • Licensing revenue for Q4 reached $12.1 million, supported by $184.1 million in licensing sales, which grew 11% year-over-year. Average weekly sales improved to $79,000 in the quarter, up from $76,000 in Q3 and Q4 2023.

  • Food and paper costs decreased by 110 basis points year-over-year, while labor-related expenses improved by 160 basis points, reflecting benefits from a new labor model introduced in Q4. Adjusted pro forma net income was $11.6 million or $0.26 per fully exchanged diluted share.

Q&A

  • Michael Tamas, Oppenheimer & Company: Asked about the confidence behind achieving 22% restaurant-level margins despite a challenging top-line environment. CFO Katie Fogertey attributed it to better labor management, supply chain efficiencies, and disciplined operations, which collectively offset headwinds.

  • Christine Cho, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the 3.7% same-Shack sales growth in January, despite weather and wildfire impacts. CFO Fogertey cited a strong underlying business supported by marketing and operational strategies.

  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: Sought clarity on the potential upside to the 22% restaurant margin target over the three-year guidance. CEO Rob Lynch explained that while the baseline reflects current execution capabilities, ongoing initiatives aim for continuous improvement despite external uncertainties.

  • Jake Bartlett, Truist Securities: Asked about the cadence of limited-time offers (LTOs). CEO Lynch noted that the current strategy involves three to four major LTO windows annually, with plans to optimize product launches to drive incremental visits and mix improvements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about Shake Shack's ability to sustain margin improvements and navigate macroeconomic headwinds. Questions regarding same-Shack sales growth, margin expansion, and operational efficiencies reflected a focus on execution risks and market uncertainties.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing operational discipline and strategic investments. CEO Rob Lynch highlighted the company's long-term growth potential, while CFO Katie Fogertey detailed specific cost-saving and productivity initiatives.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on external headwinds, such as weather impacts and inflationary pressures, while management remained consistent in showcasing confidence in its strategic priorities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Adjusted EBITDA grew significantly in Q4 2024 compared to Q3, reflecting higher restaurant-level margins and operational efficiencies. Q4 restaurant-level profit margins expanded to 22.7%, up from 21% in Q3.

  • Strategic focus shifted from building the foundational elements of loyalty and culinary calendars in Q3 to implementing operational improvements and leadership development programs in Q4.

  • Analyst sentiment showed a heightened emphasis on macroeconomic and weather-related challenges in Q4, while management showcased increased confidence in achieving 2025 targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted weather disruptions and the lingering effects of wildfires in Los Angeles as significant challenges impacting Q1 2025 performance. Additional headwinds include beef price inflation and reduced consumer discretionary spending.

  • Analysts raised concerns about execution risks tied to infill expansion and margin improvement initiatives. Management underscored its supply chain strategies and operational discipline as key mitigants.

Final Takeaway

Shake Shack delivered strong Q4 performance with robust revenue growth and expanded margins, supported by strategic operational improvements and disciplined cost management. The company provided positive 2025 guidance, targeting adjusted EBITDA of $205 million to $215 million and maintaining 22% restaurant-level margins. While external risks such as inflation and weather disruptions remain, management's confidence in its long-term growth strategy and operational enhancements positions Shake Shack for continued success.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Freshpet craters as Q4 results, outlook fail to match lofty expectations
data.description:
Freshpet (NASDAQ:FRPT) shares are under heavy selling pressure Thursday, facing its worst day since August 2022 as solid Q4 results were short of Wall Street’s ambitious expectations and a below-consensus outlook for FY25 resulted in a double-digit loss for the stock.

"Fiscal year 2024 was a breakout year for Freshpet,” said the company’s CEO Billy Cyr adding that the company delivered “exceptional” net sales growth and strong profit improvements, exceeding some of the fiscal year 2027 targets set two years ago. The company also delivered full-year positive net income for the first time and expects to be free cash flow positive by 2026.

Despite a 22% increase in sales, 68% surge in adjusted EBITDA, and 650 basis point expansion in adjusted gross margin, investors were spooked by a miss on both sales and profits, and FY25 sales guidance that missed estimates by $10M to $40M.

For FY25, the company forecasts sales to be between $1.18B and $1.21B, up from $975.2M in 2024, but less than $1.22B estimates. Adjusted EBITDA should reach at least $210M from $161.8M in 2024, and capital expenditures will reach ~$250M.

For 2027, Freshpet (NASDAQ:FRPT) raised its outlook for adjusted gross margin to 48% from 45%, and adjusted EBITDA margin to 22% from 18%, prior. Net sales are expected to hit $1.8B, unchanged from previous guidance. This is also less than $1.82B estimates.

The outsized loss for Freshpet (NASDAQ:FRPT) is spilling over into shares of Petco Health and Wellness (WOOF), and Chewy (CHWY), both of which are trading with modest losses in sympathy.

data.title: Cameco targets strong uranium production of 36M pounds in 2025 amid global demand growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cameco Corporation (NYSE:CCJ) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Tim Gitzel, CEO, highlighted the company's strong performance for both Q4 and the full year 2024, emphasizing robust results driven by Tier-1 assets and long-term contract portfolios. He stated that "we expect our strong performance to continue in 2025, supported by our long-term contract portfolio, our Tier-1 assets, and our strong financial position."

  • Gitzel emphasized favorable market conditions in the nuclear sector and increasing demand for uranium, with the company strategically positioned to grow while maintaining downside protection through long-term contracts.

  • Cameco’s uranium production for 2024 was 23.4 million pounds, with 34 million pounds delivered. The McArthur River/Key Lake operation achieved a record 20.3 million packaged pounds, attributed to investments in automation and digitization during care and maintenance.

  • CFO Grant Isaac noted that Cameco's long-term uranium contract portfolio now totals approximately 220 million pounds, with commitments to deliver an average of 28 million pounds annually over the next five years, higher in 2025-2027 and lower in subsequent years.

Outlook

  • Cameco plans to produce 36 million pounds of uranium in 2025, split evenly between McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake at 18 million pounds each. Production at Inkai remains uncertain due to ongoing discussions with JV Inkai and Kazatomprom.

  • Gitzel indicated that Cameco is well-positioned for future production flexibility, aligning supply with its contract book and market opportunities. Investments in infrastructure reliability and sustainability at Key Lake and McArthur River are ongoing.

  • Westinghouse, a key acquisition for Cameco, is expected to achieve 6%-10% adjusted EBITDA growth annually over the next five years, with opportunities in AP1000 reactor builds and collaborations with Korea.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 reflected strong operational performance, including higher sales volumes and improved realized pricing. The uranium segment saw higher-than-expected production at McArthur River/Key Lake, offsetting lower output at Inkai and Cigar Lake.

  • Inkai’s production in 2024 was impacted by sulfuric acid supply chain challenges, with total production reaching 7.8 million pounds, compared to 8.3 million pounds expected.

  • Cameco ended 2024 with a strong balance sheet, fully repaying the $600 million U.S. floating rate term loan used for the Westinghouse acquisition and refinancing $500 million in unsecured debt.

Q&A

  • Adam Wijaya, Goldman Sachs: Asked about contracting activity trends. CFO Grant Isaac responded that while 2024 reported term volumes were lower than 2023, term prices increased significantly, signaling robust demand and tight supply dynamics. Isaac emphasized patience in contracting to ensure favorable pricing.

  • Andrew Wong, RBC Capital Markets: Inquired about the impact of the Westinghouse-KHNP settlement on opportunities. Isaac noted expanded collaboration with Korean entities, which could open new markets and projects, including in the UAE and Czech Republic.

  • Alexander Pearce, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about McArthur River production guidance. Gitzel confirmed that production would return to 18 million pounds annually, with potential increases aligned to contract demand.

  • Orest Wowkodaw, Scotiabank: Raised concerns about U.S. tariffs on Canadian uranium. Gitzel stated that Cameco's newer contracts allocate tariff costs to customers, minimizing financial impact, and highlighted the company’s ability to position material strategically.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on the company's ability to capitalize on favorable market conditions while navigating risks like geopolitical issues and production uncertainties at Inkai. Questions often sought clarity on production guidance and contracting trends.

  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, showcasing strategic planning and resilience. Statements like "we are confident" and "we have positioned ourselves to benefit" underscored their optimism.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more bullish, reflecting the strong operational results and favorable market conditions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 production remained consistent with a focus on disciplined supply management. The Q3 call also emphasized strong performance, but Q4 highlighted record production achievements at McArthur River/Key Lake.

  • Analysts' focus shifted from general market dynamics in Q3 to specific concerns about tariffs, Inkai production, and contracting trends in Q4.

  • Management confidence improved, supported by tangible operational achievements and strategic positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Production at Inkai remains uncertain due to sulfuric acid supply challenges and regulatory issues. Discussions with Kazatomprom are ongoing to finalize 2025 production allocations.

  • Potential U.S. tariffs on Canadian uranium could impact market dynamics. Management has proactively adjusted contracts and positioned material to mitigate risks.

  • The uranium market remains tight, with utilities delaying long-term contracting decisions, potentially creating pricing volatility.

Final Takeaway

Cameco delivered a strong Q4 2024 performance, supported by record production at McArthur River/Key Lake and a robust long-term contract portfolio. The company is targeting 36 million pounds of uranium production in 2025, while addressing risks such as Inkai production uncertainties and potential U.S. tariffs. Westinghouse remains a growth driver, with opportunities in reactor builds and market expansion. Cameco’s strategic positioning and disciplined approach to contracting underscore its confidence in capturing value from favorable market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Walmart outlines FY26 EPS guidance of $2.50-$2.60 with focus on e-commerce and membership growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Walmart Inc. (NYSE:WMT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Doug McMillon highlighted Walmart's strong Q4 performance, with 5.2% sales growth and a 9.4% increase in adjusted operating income in constant currency. He emphasized market share gains across income levels and countries, noting strength in general merchandise and higher-margin businesses such as membership, marketplace, and advertising.
  • McMillon announced the upcoming IPO preparation for PhonePe, Walmart's fintech business in India, which aims to expand its market presence.
  • CFO John David Rainey outlined consolidated revenue growth of 5.6% for the full year in constant currency, with adjusted EPS up 13%. He emphasized the growth of Walmart's digital businesses, including a 27% increase in global advertising revenue and a 21% rise in global membership income.
  • Rainey also highlighted a 20% reduction in U.S. net delivery costs per order, driven by delivery densification and increased penetration of paid expedited delivery options.

Outlook

  • Walmart projects fiscal 2026 consolidated net sales growth of 3%-4%, with operating income growing faster at 3.5%-5.5%. Adjusted EPS is expected to range between $2.50 and $2.60.
  • For Q1 FY26, Walmart expects net sales growth of 3%-4% in constant currency and operating income growth of 0.5%-2%.
  • The company anticipates currency headwinds of approximately 100 basis points to sales growth and 150 basis points to operating income growth in FY26.

Financial Results

  • Q4 consolidated revenue grew over 5% in constant currency, with e-commerce sales up 16%. Walmart U.S. comparable sales rose 4.6%, driven by a 20% increase in e-commerce sales and mid-single-digit growth in grocery.
  • Gross margins expanded by 53 basis points, supported by better inventory management and lower markdowns.
  • Global membership income increased by 16% in Q4, while Walmart U.S. Marketplace revenue grew 37%, with nearly 45% of orders fulfilled by Walmart Fulfillment Services.
  • Adjusted EPS for Q4 came in at $0.66, compared to analysts’ estimate of $0.65. Consolidated revenues totaled $178.9 billion, meeting analysts’ expectations.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Questioned Walmart's economic sensitivity and the impact on FY26 guidance. CEO Doug McMillon and CFO John David Rainey emphasized the company's strong value proposition and broader profit streams, noting a 5%-7% adjusted EBIT growth outlook after adjusting for leap year and VIZIO acquisition impacts.
  • Kate McShane, Goldman Sachs: Asked about gross margin mix and general merchandise growth. Management highlighted improving general merchandise sales and incremental contributions from marketplace and advertising businesses.
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about reinvestment rates and e-commerce margins. CFO Rainey noted an 11% incremental margin in e-commerce and emphasized balancing profit expansion with long-term investments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to positive tone, raising questions on profitability, guidance, and reinvestment strategies. Concerns primarily revolved around economic sensitivity and gross margin impacts.
  • Management demonstrated confidence in their strategy, emphasizing consistent execution and profitability growth. Phrases such as "we feel really good about our ability to navigate" and "we're in a great spot to start the year" reflected optimism.
  • The tone was consistent with the previous quarter, with management showing increased confidence in e-commerce profitability and new revenue streams.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw a slight deceleration in sales growth compared to Q3, which benefited from one-time events such as hurricanes. However, adjusted EPS growth remained robust.
  • Marketplace revenue growth accelerated from 42% in Q3 to 37% in Q4, while global advertising growth remained strong at 27% for the year.
  • Management's tone remained consistent, with a strong focus on e-commerce profitability and alternative revenue streams.
  • Analysts' questions continued to center on margins, reinvestment, and economic sensitivity, reflecting similar themes from the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged uncertainties related to macroeconomic conditions, currency fluctuations, and geopolitical risks.
  • Rising costs, including wage investments and marketing expenses, were highlighted as potential pressures on margins.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the sustainability of general merchandise growth and potential headwinds from inflation and tariffs.

Final Takeaway

Walmart delivered another quarter of solid performance, highlighted by strong sales and profitability growth, driven by gains in e-commerce, advertising, and membership income. The company issued a cautious yet confident FY26 outlook, emphasizing long-term investments in digital capabilities and alternative revenue streams. Analysts remained focused on gross margin pressures, reinvestment strategies, and economic sensitivity, while management maintained a positive tone, signaling strong operational momentum and market share gains across income cohorts and categories.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon, SLB plan meetings in Chile to pursue lithium projects - Reuters
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) is planning a meeting with officials in Chile to discuss lithium opportunities, Reuters reported late Wednesday, as fossil fuel companies increasingly look to invest in production of the metal needed for electric vehicle batteries.

Top U.S. oilfield services company SLB (SLB), which also is expanding into lithium, also recently met with Chilean mining officials, according to the report.

A Chilean representative for Exxon (NYSE:XOM) reportedly met last month with mining ministry officials to discuss an upcoming visit by executives "interested in lithium projects in Chile," according to a registry of lobbyist meetings.

Exxon (XOM) has been working to use so-called direct lithium extraction, which separates the metal from brine, a method that Chile has endorsed as a way to mitigate environmental impacts.

Also in January, SLB (SLB) Head of Mining Nicholas Lugansky reportedly met with a top Chilean mining official to discuss the company's New Energy unit, which includes lithium projects.

SLB (SLB) already is one of eight companies the government has selected to test its lithium extraction technology in the Altoandinos salt flats in northern Chile.

Lithium production in Chile currently comes from just two companies in the Atacama salt flat: SQM (SQM) and Albemarle (ALB).

data.title: American Water projects 8% EPS growth in 2025 with $3.3B capital investment plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Water Works Company, Inc. (NYSE:AWK) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Susan Hardwick announced her upcoming retirement effective May 14, 2025, with John Griffith set to take over as CEO. This transition is part of the company’s succession plan initiated in August 2024.
  • John Griffith highlighted that 2024 EPS reached $5.39, including favorable weather impacts and incremental interest income. The company invested over $3 billion in capital, achieving its 2% compounded annual growth in customer connections through acquisitions.
  • CFO David Bowler reported $5.39 EPS for 2024, reflecting a $0.49 increase from 2023. Key drivers included rate increases, acquisitions, and customer demand changes, partially offset by higher operating and production costs.
  • Griffith affirmed 2025 EPS guidance of $5.65 to $5.75, signaling 8% growth driven by rate base expansion and operational execution. He emphasized the company’s low-risk, top-tier growth plan supported by infrastructure renewal.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed 2025 EPS guidance of $5.65 to $5.75, representing 8% growth from weather-normalized 2024 earnings. The company also committed to maintaining a 7% to 9% EPS growth trajectory through 2029, supported by 8% to 9% rate base growth.
  • Capital investments for 2025 are projected at $3.3 billion, part of a $17 billion to $18 billion five-year plan.
  • The company anticipates issuing $1 billion in equity in 2026 and $1.5 billion in 2029 to fund growth and maintain financial stability.

Financial Results

  • Full-year 2024 EPS reached $5.39, benefiting from favorable weather impacts and incremental interest income. Revenues increased due to rate adjustments, acquisitions, and customer demand changes, net of higher production and operating costs.
  • The company successfully completed seven rate cases in 2024, including $105 million in Illinois and $53 million in California, with new rates effective in 2025.
  • Operating and maintenance expenses increased by $0.22 per share, driven by employee costs and production-related expenses. Depreciation and financing costs also rose, reflecting ongoing capital investments.

Q&A

  • Durgesh Chopra, Evercore ISI: Asked about PFAS investment strategy and its alignment with regulatory developments. Cheryl Norton confirmed no changes to the $1 billion PFAS-related capital plan.
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Inquired about the company’s expanded business development capabilities. Norton highlighted enhanced staffing and processes driving growth across multiple states.
  • Angie Storozynski, Seaport: Questioned equity financing strategies amid water sector valuation challenges. Bowler stated hybrids were not cost-effective and reaffirmed traditional equity issuance plans.
  • Jonathan Reeder, Wells Fargo: Asked about settlement expectations in Missouri rate cases. Griffith indicated the company anticipates reaching a settlement before hearings begin.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral to slightly positive sentiment, focusing on the company’s stable growth trajectory and capital investment plans. Questions centered on regulatory developments, financing, and customer growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Statements such as “we expect to achieve consistent EPS growth” and “we are confident in our financing plan” reflected assurance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone showed consistent confidence, while analysts remained focused on execution and regulatory outcomes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, reaffirming 2025 EPS growth and long-term targets. The capital investment plan increased to $17 billion to $18 billion over five years, up by $1 billion from Q3 projections.
  • Analysts maintained a focus on financing strategies, regulatory developments, and customer growth, similar to Q3. However, Q4 included heightened interest in equity issuance timing and its impact on earnings.
  • Management sentiment remained steady, emphasizing execution and low-risk growth plans, while analysts continued to probe regulatory and financing considerations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified regulatory risks tied to rate case outcomes and expectations for timely cost recovery. The company is seeking improvements in regulatory mechanisms in jurisdictions like Missouri.
  • Analysts raised concerns about equity dilution and the impact of rising production costs, particularly electricity expenses. Management highlighted long-term contracts to mitigate electricity price risks.

Final Takeaway

American Water Works continues to execute its long-term growth strategy, reaffirming an 8% EPS growth target for 2025 and projecting consistent 7% to 9% growth through 2029. The company’s $3.3 billion capital investment for 2025, supported by rate base expansion and acquisitions, underscores its commitment to infrastructure renewal and customer service. With regulatory processes and financing strategies well-aligned, the company remains positioned as a low-risk, premium utility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Piedmont Lithium targets 113,000–130,000 tons in 2025 shipments amid merger with Sayona
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Piedmont Lithium Inc. (NASDAQ:PLL) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Keith Phillips highlighted strong operational performance at North American Lithium (NAL), with nearly 51,000 tons produced in Q4 2024 and over 190,000 tons for the year. He stated that improved lithium recoveries and increased mill utilization drove these results, supported by investments in infrastructure like the crushed ore storage dome.
  • The company announced a merger with joint venture partner Sayona Mining, positioning the combined entity as North America’s largest lithium producer. The merger aims to unlock synergies of $15 million to $20 million annually and expand production capabilities.
  • CFO Michael White reported $45.6 million in Q4 revenue, driven by record shipments of 55,700 tons. He noted that realized prices per metric ton were $818, with an SC6 equivalent price of $909.
  • White also emphasized the company’s focus on cost containment, achieving $14 million in annual cost savings through restructuring efforts and operational efficiencies.

Outlook

  • The company anticipates shipping 25,000 to 30,000 dry metric tons in Q1 2025, with full-year shipments expected to range between 113,000 and 130,000 tons. This includes shipments deferred into 2025 due to customer requests.
  • Capital expenditures for 2025 are projected between $6 million and $9 million, with joint venture investments estimated at $7 million to $13 million for the year.
  • Phillips expressed optimism about obtaining air and water permits for the Carolina Lithium project in 2025, which are critical for advancing the project.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net loss in Q4 was $3.6 million, or a loss of $0.17 per share. GAAP net loss was $11.1 million, including $5.5 million in transaction costs related to the merger and $3.2 million in restructuring charges.
  • The company ended the year with $87.8 million in cash, up from $64.4 million in the previous quarter, supported by $25 million in proceeds from an ASX-listed capital raise and $7 million in additional borrowings.
  • Operating cash flows for the quarter were negative $6 million, while full-year cash outflows totaled $43 million, largely due to prior year spot sales settlements.

Q&A

  • Bill Peterson, JPMorgan, asked about potential impacts of tariffs on shipments to the U.S. Phillips noted that tariffs could increase costs for American customers but are unlikely to affect broader sales, as most shipments are directed to non-U.S. regions.
  • Joseph Reagor, ROTH Capital, inquired about merger timing and remaining hurdles. Phillips estimated mid-2025 completion, citing SEC review as the primary determinant. He expressed confidence in obtaining shareholder approval.
  • Greg Jones, BMO, questioned the development timeline for the Ewoyaa project in Ghana. Phillips emphasized that market conditions will dictate the pace, with 2025 focused on permitting and funding preparations.
  • Noel Parks, Tuohy Brothers, explored partnerships for processing in Quebec. Phillips highlighted potential benefits of regional processing to reduce transportation costs and improve margins.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a neutral tone, with questions focused on shipping, market conditions, and project timelines. Concerns centered on the timing of shipments and the market’s impact on investment decisions.
  • Management maintained a confident yet cautious tone. Phillips described the lithium market as volatile but expressed optimism about long-term demand growth and the company’s strategic positioning. He emphasized cost management and careful capital deployment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained consistent, with a continued focus on operational efficiencies and market adaptability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q4 2024 was revised upward to reflect record shipments and improved revenue. Q4 shipments exceeded Q3 levels by 24,200 tons, driving a $17.9 million increase in revenue.
  • Cost reductions were more pronounced in Q4, with a 20% decline in unit costs per ton from the year’s start, compared to an 11% decline in Q3.
  • Management’s tone in Q4 was slightly more optimistic, supported by merger developments and operational achievements.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from operational ramp-ups in Q3 to strategic initiatives and market impacts in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated mining costs due to underground workings at NAL remain a near-term challenge, though improvements are expected by 2026.
  • Uncertainty in lithium prices could delay project timelines, particularly for the Ewoyaa and Carolina Lithium projects.
  • Potential tariffs on critical minerals imported to the U.S. could impact customer decisions and shipping logistics.
  • The company highlighted its strategy to mitigate risks through cost savings, selective hedging, and strategic partnerships.

Final Takeaway

Piedmont Lithium’s Q4 2024 earnings call emphasized strong operational results, cost-saving measures, and strategic initiatives, including a merger with Sayona Mining. Management projected steady shipment growth in 2025 while maintaining a cautious approach to capital deployment amid volatile lithium markets. Analysts raised questions about tariffs, project timelines, and market impacts, reflecting a balanced sentiment. The company’s focus on synergies, cost control, and leveraging market opportunities positions it for long-term growth despite near-term challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Electromed is a new Buy at B. Riley; Buyout potential cited
data.description:
B. Riley launched its coverage of Electromed (OTCQB:EMED) with a Buy recommendation and a $38 per share target, arguing that the company, being the final remaining pure-play stock in a market for a type of airway clearance products, is a potential takeout target.

The New Prague, Minnesota-based firm offers high-frequency chest wall oscillation (HFCWO) airway clearance products targeting patients with respiratory disorders such as COPD in a market worth about $240M in annual sales, according to B. Riley’s Kyle Bauser.

With its rivals being healthcare conglomerates such as Baxter (BAX) and Phillips (PHG), EMED “is the final remaining pure-play in the HFCWO space, making it an attractive takeout target, in our opinion, for a larger medtech strategy looking to leverage the pulmonologist call point,” Bauser said.

Additionally, the analyst expects the company to continue its double-digit revenue growth and maintain its sizable operating leverage in the pursuit of $1.00 of per share earnings by fiscal 2026 and 20% operating margins over time.
